content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Most developers only find out they were attacked after the damage is done. Here’s what to look for — and how to read it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sleep better we’re awakeMost PHP developers never look at their logs until something breaks.
By then, it’s too late.
A SQL injection attack doesn’t announce itself. There’s no alarm. No warning email. No red flag on your dashboard. One moment your app is running normally. The next, an attacker has extracted your entire users table, your client’s personal data, or your payment records — and you have no idea it happened.
Here’s what that attack actually looked like in your logs while it was happening.
What a SQL Injection Attempt Looks Like in Raw Logs
A typical Apache or Nginx access log entry looks like this during a normal request:
192.168.1.1 - - [04/Jul/2026:08:23:11 +0000] "GET /users?id=1 HTTP/1.1" 200 512Clean. Readable. Nothing suspicious.
Now here’s what a SQL injection attempt looks like in the same log:
185.220.101.45 - - [04/Jul/2026:08:23:47 +0000] "GET /users?id=1' OR '1'='1 HTTP/1.1" 200 8921Three things stand out immediately if you know what to look for:
1. The IP address is different.
185.220.101.45 is a known Tor exit node. Attackers route through Tor and VPNs to mask their origin. A sudden request from an IP you’ve never seen before, especially one hitting a query parameter, deserves attention.
2. The payload in the URL.
id=1' OR '1'='1 is one of the oldest SQL injection strings in existence. The attacker is trying to modify your SQL query by injecting their own logic. If your code passes this directly into a database query without sanitization, the query becomes:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=1 OR '1'='1'Which returns every row in your users table. Every single one.
3. The response size jumped.
Normal request returned 512 bytes. This request returned 8,921 bytes. That’s not normal. That’s your database talking too much.
The Attack Sequence: What the Full Log Shows
SQL injection attacks rarely come as a single request. They come in sequences. Here’s what a real attack sequence looks like across 10 minutes of logs:
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1'" 500 — # Testing for error
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1 AND 1=1" 200 512 # Confirming injection point
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1 AND 1=2" 200 0 # Boolean blind test
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1 ORDER BY 1--" 200 512 # Counting columns
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1 ORDER BY 5--" 500 — # Finding column limit
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=1 UNION SELECT null,null,null,null--" 200 512 # Union test
185.220.101.45 "GET /users?id=-1 UNION SELECT 1,username,password,email FROM users--" 200 9841 # Data extractionThat last request. 9,841 bytes returned. That is your users table being extracted.
The entire sequence took 8 minutes. Most developers would never notice it unless they were watching their logs in real time.
Why Most PHP Developers Miss This
There are three reasons this kind of attack goes undetected:
1. Logs are not monitored in real time.
Most developers check logs reactively — after a bug report, after a complaint, after something breaks. Attackers know this. They move faster than your reaction time.
2. The response code is 200.
Your server returned a 200 OK on the attack request. From your server’s perspective, the request succeeded. Nothing looked wrong at the HTTP layer. The damage happened at the database layer.
3. The payload is URL-encoded in most logs.
In production, that SQL injection string often arrives encoded:
id=1%27%20OR%20%271%27%3D%271Unless you know to decode it, it looks like noise.
What to Add to Your PHP App Right Now
If you want basic visibility into what’s hitting your application, add this to your PHP error logging:
// Log suspicious query parameters
$suspicious = ["'", "union", "select", "drop", "insert", "--", "/*"];
$query = strtolower($_SERVER['QUERY_STRING'] ?? '');foreach ($suspicious as $pattern) {
if (strpos($query, $pattern) !== false) {
error_log("SUSPICIOUS REQUEST: " . $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'] . " — " . $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']);
break;
}
}
This won’t stop an attack. But it will at least tell you one is happening.
The real fix is parameterized queries — always, without exception:
// Never do this
$query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " . $_GET['id'];// Always do this
$stmt = $pdo->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");
$stmt->execute([$_GET['id']]);
Parameterized queries make SQL injection structurally impossible for that query. The user input is never interpreted as SQL.
What Changes When You Have Real Visibility
The problem with the manual approach above is that you’re still reacting. You’re reading logs after the fact, adding pattern matching after you’ve already been probed, and hoping you catch the next one before it succeeds.
Real visibility means knowing the moment a suspicious request hits your app not an hour later when you check the logs, not a day later when you notice your database is larger than it should be.
I built Kriosa specifically because this problem kept hitting PHP and Laravel developers around me with no affordable way to see it in real time. The dashboard shows every suspicious request the moment it arrives, explains exactly what pattern triggered the detection, and gives you the confidence score behind the decision — not just “blocked,” but why.
If you’re running a PHP or Laravel app and you’ve never looked at what’s actually hitting it, the logs above might be running right now.
Try it free: kriosa.com
Install it: composer require kriosa-ai/kriosa-php
Built by a developer who got tired of watching others get hacked with no way to see it coming.
